item.title: Starmer says some road and energy projects will be scrapped to fund £15bn defence plan
item.description: Publication of long-delayed blueprint is unlikely to end military figures’ calls for more funding

item.title: Apple and Google app store fees targeted by UK competition watchdog
item.description: CMA regulator launches consultation on whether Big Tech groups should allow rival payments

item.title: Comcast’s ‘amicable divorce’ is latest episode in Hollywood drama
item.description: NBCUniversal split sparks talk of future deals amid ongoing media and entertainment consolidation

item.title: EY employee charged with accessing Australian prime minister’s bank details
item.description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers describes situation as ‘incredibly concerning’

item.title: Barclays buys Canary Wharf HQ for £750mn
item.description: UK bank buys 999-year lease on London headquarters in one of Europe’s biggest office deals in recent years

item.title: Sainsbury’s sales growth slows as consumer caution hits retailers
item.description: Rival supermarket Tesco also reports slowdown

item.title: Scottish Power calls on regulator to securitise bad UK energy debts
item.description: Household energy debts hit £4.8bn in the UK, adding more than £50 a year to the average bill

item.title: FT Alphaville’s AI Prediction World Cup: Post-group update: Predictions are predictably hard
item.description: Many stats; no learnings

item.title: EU must choose which sectors to protect or face exodus, warns Covestro chief
item.description: Comments come as Abu Dhabi-owned German group announces investments of up to €4bn in China and the UAE

item.title: FirstFT: How Elliott spawned a new army of activists
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: corporate AI users’ staff hires and Maersk raises profit guidance

item.title: Paul Singer’s activist hedge fund factory
item.description: Elliott Management alums have founded at least seven hedge funds since 2020

item.title: Car finance lenders say UK compensation scheme violates their human rights
item.description: Customer payouts on hold as groups including Volkswagen Financial Services and Crédit Agricole launch legal challenge

item.title: What would multilateral ‘AI arms control’ look like?
item.description: Given the competition, it’s debatable whether a US-China safety deal is even possible

item.title: Magnificent Seven stocks shed $2.3tn in Wall Street tech rotation
item.description: Investors switch to soaring chipmakers benefiting from hyperscalers’ vast AI spending

item.title: Heavy corporate AI spenders add staff faster than peers
item.description: Study of 22,000 US companies challenges fears that generative AI will trigger broad job losses

item.title: Wildfarmed is turning regenerative agriculture into a brand
item.description: The grain business founded by Andy Cato of Groove Armada is going mainstream

item.title: Is AI an exoskeleton for the mind?
item.description: Technology that helps people do things they couldn’t otherwise achieve can also lead to atrophy

item.title: Elliott’s acolytes: how Paul Singer’s hedge fund became a spinout factory
item.description: The vaunted investor has spawned a small army of new firms mirroring Julian Robertson’s ‘Tiger cubs’

item.title: The great wealth transfer rattling Wall Street
item.description: Tens of trillions of dollars are moving between generations — and younger heirs feel little loyalty to traditional advisers

item.title: Quarter of UK water groups underspend investment allowances as network creaks
item.description: Utilities failed to use maximum allowed despite arguing that higher bills were needed to fund improvements

item.title: Private equity fund investors turn to debt-like deals in downturn
item.description: Backers of buyout funds agreed $9bn worth of ‘alternative’ transactions last year, up from $6bn in 2024

item.title: Private developers lag behind public sector in fixing cladding issues
item.description: Replacing dangerous material could take a decade as there is ‘no incentive to complete’, says new research

item.title: AI in Practice
item.description: How artificial intelligence is being set to use in — and often fundamentally changing — multiple sectors, including: budget-busting AI bills; transforming pharma; AI and trust in air traffic control; spreading factory automation; rewriting gaming rules; chatbots and mental health; astronomical opportunities; agentic travel agents

item.title: AI speeds the march of China’s factory robots into new sectors
item.description: Artificial intelligence is enabling the spread of automation to traditional industries

item.title: How AI and an astronomer’s laptop can bring new galaxies within reach
item.description: AI is spotting patterns in fresh and old data with surprisingly little computing power

item.title: Holiday industry prepares for the agentic travel agent
item.description: Chatbots could help to find, filter and book customers’ destinations

item.title: Can AI help avoid an air traffic control crisis — and would we trust it?
item.description: The technology has potential to assist controllers with an increasing flight load but many are wary

item.title: Why AI can transform pharma — but nature cannot be hurried
item.description: Artificial intelligence has the potential to fundamentally change the development of new treatments

item.title: Businesses face up to budget-busting AI bills
item.description: A shift to usage-based pricing and new models is making companies rethink spending

item.title: AI drives a boom in new games but big developers dominate
item.description: More instinctive technology is accelerating production amid concerns it risks losing gamers’ trust
